speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: To be quiet. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: What is that exactly? He's going to drive me up a wall what kind of a film. I'm going to tell them a film about noisy neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, tell him. [LAUGHTER] Did you feel that you told most of the stories that you wanted?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the, the main, the main thing was--

speaker: interviewer
text: The identification now, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, do you want me to say something to introduce it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, just-- yeah, but you'll have to-- just, just come near this microphone here. You can say it into-- no, in here. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Into here? OK. Freda, we have the map of Transnistria in front of us. If you could just locate the main areas, please--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. You just announce this is tape four.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four with Freda Rosenblatt.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Romania, [POINTING] this part. This is Bessarabia, who before the war, was part of Romania, too. This is the Ukraine. All of it, actually, is Ukraine. But this part was made Transnistria during the war, from '41 to '44. This is the River Dniester. This is the River Bug. And between the two rivers, this is Transnistria. This is the Black Sea and Odessa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Freda, could you locate your hometown and then the route which you followed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I will born in this town, [? Yedenetz, ?] right here. In 1941, we moved to Czernowitz. From Czernowitz, when the war started, we were deported from Czernowitz by train to Marculesti. From Marculesti, we were forced to march Cosauti Forest, cross the River Dniester, Yampil, Kryzhopil, Obodovka, Bershad. And from Bershad, in '43, we were taken the orphans to Balta, and then Balta we're going back to Bershad. And there, we're liberated from the Russians and marching all home to Yedenetz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Freda has agreed to share some of the photographs with us. Freda, would you explain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, rather than pointing, say on the right and on the-- OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I see. Oh, yeah, this is the right. I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: The pen, it kicks up a shadow, that's why.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I see. I'm on, I'm on, I'm on the right side?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you're on the left. As you're looking at it, as you look at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You on the left-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- I'm left, my brother's right, to be again, on the safe side. Won't mix up.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Do ?] [? you ?] [? want ?] [? me ?] to say it again? Freda's agreed to share some of her photographs with us. Freda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this, in the left, it's me, a little girl. I don't know-- I don't, sure, I don't remember how old we're in there. And on the, on the right is my brother, childhood pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would be around about 1934, '35?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately in the, in the early '30s, or mid-'30s approximately. This is myself. I must have been there seven years old or so. I think this must have been one of the last pictures, because I really don't remember taking any after this.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, this is, in the left side, it's me dancing in kindergarten with a friend of mine, [? Ozzy ?] [? Ziegman. ?] She is in Calgary. I haven't seen her since 1946, but we talk for-- occasionally on the telephone and write each others letters-- in kindergarten, both about six years old or so. This is my brother playing on the violin. He started to play when he was five years old. On this picture, he must be about seven years old. He finished two years conservatorium before the war. The violin was taken away by him in Marculesti by the Romanian gendarmes. This is my family-- my father, my mother, myself, and my brother. This also must be taken before the war, the late '30s, because I don't have any other pictures. I don't think anything later was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: She go through the people are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just introduce them.

speaker: interviewee
text: The left is my father. On the right side is my mother. My brother stands behind. And I'm right in the front in the white coat, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] special outfits.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it moving in?

speaker: interviewer
text: --just remember my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Where is it going?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right side is myself. And I'm with my mother. This was in the Carpathian Mountains. We used to go for a vacation. You can see the mountains in the background. We used to go quite often there for a vacation.

speaker: interviewer
text: My brother used to go with my mother to the Black Sea. Why? I don't know. But I used to go to the mountain, my brother to the sea. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the kindergarten. My brother, he's on the fourth in the middle from the night. This book was actually done by the survivors from Yedenetz from Transnistria. They get all those pictures. And they put together a book in the memory of the people from Yedenetz that didn't survive, and for the people who are alive to remember what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: My relatives send me from Israel about 20 years ago, because they were on the committee of making this book. And that they send me. For me, it's very precious because everything what happened-- before the war, after the war, and during the war, what happened with the Jews from Yedenetz, and Bessarabia, and Transnistria, very much detailed, even things that I even I don't, didn't knew, didn't remember, it's in this book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Freda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This picture is about a Purim carnival, also in Yedenetz, before the war. And it's also in the same book from Yedenetz. The picture is in the same book from Yedenetz, also in the memory. Hardly-- I don't think anybody survived from this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's OK. This is near the River Dniester just before the Jews is to be taken over to Transnistria on the other side of the river. This picture was actually taken by Romanian soldier, that he was at that time there. And he was good enough to give it to the Jewish Archives, which are in Israel. This somebody made actually a drawing, how it looked, old, young, people with children, forced march. That's how it looked. Parents holding their children, their loved ones, older people hardly could walk. That's how it looked, the forced marching in Transnistria. This picture is a oil painting made by somebody what's now in Brazil to demonstrate how the people looked when they broken down, marching from place to place with snow on the ground in winter, in the cold. This is the entrance of the cemetery. They set out the monument for the-- this is the monument in the lager Bershad in the memory of the thousands and thousands who died in Bershad. Also, my parents lie in that cemetery in mass grave, which I never know exactly where it is. But after the war, some Jews erected this beautiful monument in the memory of them. Well, this is my wedding day to my husband of 41 years, the happiest day in our lives, Shlomo Rosenblatt and Freda Rosenblatt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date? What year? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 19-- February 22nd, 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, I wouldn't dare, if I know what-- if I know what's good for me. In the left side, this is my son, Henry. Right now, he's going to be 38 years old next month, actually two months-- and his wife, Rebecca. And this is on their wedding day. On the left side is my son-in-law, Robert [? Manson. ?] And in the right side, sure is my daughter Sarah Rosenblatt [? Manson. ?] On the left side is my youngest son-in-law, Michael Pataznik. And on the right is my younger daughter, Pnina Rosenblatt Pataznik.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is my grandson, my younger one, Joshua Rosenblatt. And he's 6 and 1/2 years old. This is my older grandson, David Rosenblatt. And he's eight and 1/2 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who does he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like his daddy. And I wouldn't say-- [LAUGHS] This is my brother in a Hebrew high school in another city, Belz. This also must be one of the last pictures with the uniform from high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hersch Solter.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] to the family [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is, this is me in about 1948. So it was three years after the war. The sadness and the pain in my eyes, I'm-- even I'm 16 years old, I look like a child lost from pain and misery. Well, I would like to introduce my family. This my husband, Shlomo Rosenblatt. And, and this is my daughter, Pnina Rosenblatt Pataznik.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Whatever you saw in this film, I can say only a few words. But I am the luckiest guy to marry this lady, this survivor kid. She's a brilliant person. And such a people had to be perished in such a way by the Nazis. And we are married for over 40 years now. And we're very happy, married. And we have children. And I hope that the world will be peaceful not to destroy such a good people. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to say how, how pleased I am to have two wonderful parents. While you've just heard my mother's story, my father is also a survivor of the Holocaust. It has an incredible impact on the generations thereafter. And I'll just touch very briefly on that. As a survivor of the, of Holocaust, as a, as a child survivor, you don't have very many relatives to speak of, generally. You have your immediate family. And that's about it. Listening to my mother discuss what happened to her family, talking about her brother, et cetera, is very painful for, for her children as well. For starters, if you took a look at the pictures of all of us, you can see something in each one of us that's a reflection of either one of her parents or her brother, as an example. So it's extremely painful for all of us. We've been watching our mother survive and live the tragedy day after day after day. She's dealing with it. We're incredibly proud of her, of her courage, to, to talk, to talk about it with her family to ensure that we all know, and that we all understand, and that we don't forget, and that, God willing, our children's children won't forget. And they will remember as well. It's incredibly unfortunate. We all live with it. And as I mentioned, I'm very proud of her. I'm proud of my father as well. He, thank God, didn't live through the same ordeal as my mother. But you know, thank God he has some of his family left. The other thing I would like to state, God willing, my mother's brother is still alive somewhere. She doesn't know what happened to him after the war, as, as, as is the case in, in many families. I'm hoping that, perhaps, through this video, maybe someone that knew of him or perhaps he himself will see this video and maybe there will be a reunion. I, I pray for this. And I hope that this video will provide this as well. This is a commemoration for all of us, for our families. We thank you. We, your children, I can speak on behalf of all of us. Unfortunately, Henry and Sarah can't be here. Your story will never be forgotten. We will carry on the story for you. We promise you that. And thank you for the gift of this video so that we may do so. And then, finally, I'd like to thank the producers of such videos for giving the survivors the chance to have this kind of tool in our possession so that we won't forget, and so that the generations thereafter won't forget. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to recite two poems. They were written in Transnistria. The writer is unknown. People used to use their talents to write poems, songs to the terrible unfortunate times. My first poem is practically about Transnistria. It's in Yiddish. [YIDDISH SPEECH] My second poem is about an orphan that says goodbye at the grave of his parents. [YIDDISH SPEECH]
